event_summary: As Trump departed for Beijing on May 13, 2026, the Strait of Hormuz remained effectively closed; Trump rejected Iran's latest counter-proposal as 'totally unacceptable,' and the IEA reported global oil stocks falling 117 million barrels in April alone.
tldr: The most striking finding is the extreme isolation of Spain (0.398) and Qatar (0.432) — two buckets with zero exclusive vocabulary, suggesting their isolation is thematic rather than linguistic. The ECONOMIC frame dominates the broadest coalition (10+ buckets), but beneath it a POLITICAL sub-story about the Trump-Xi summit's inverted leverage dynamics runs through Canadian, South African, and opinion-magazine coverage in ways absent from Asian or Middle Eastern press. Iran opposition press focuses almost exclusively on domestic repression — executions and an internet shutdown now in its 75th day — while the rest of the corpus barely notices. The QUALITY_OF_LIFE frame appears only in Russian state media and state_tv_intl, where Indonesian fishermen stranded by diesel costs humanise what other buckets treat as an aggregate supply shock.

isolation_top.note: Zero exclusive vocabulary; thematic isolation rather than linguistic. Spanish coverage likely emphasises the European energy-shortage angle distinct from Anglophone and Arab framings.
Zero exclusive vocabulary; Qatar's unique position as both LNG supplier and diplomatic intermediary may produce a framing mix not shared by any other single bucket.
Exclusive vocab 'fossil' — Ukrainian coverage likely connects the Hormuz disruption to European fossil-fuel dependency and Russia's energy leverage, a frame absent elsewhere.
RT-dominated; QUALITY_OF_LIFE frame (Indonesian fishermen, Nigerian oil curse) is unique to this bucket — a 'Global South victim' narrative not found in Western or Arab press.

bottom_line: The ECONOMIC frame achieves the broadest cross-regional consensus in this corpus, but two counter-narratives run in parallel without touching: Iran opposition press documents 75 days of internet shutdown and fresh executions while the rest of the world watches the Strait, and Russian state media humanises the crisis through Indonesian fishing communities while every other bucket treats it as an aggregate supply number. The most consequential framing divergence is on Trump-Xi leverage: Canadian and opinion-magazine press frames Trump as the weaker party entering Beijing, while Asian and Middle Eastern press report the summit without assigning relative leverage.
